title: Lifting and preserving state

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Lifting and preserving state; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Lifting and preserving state
sources.claim: Shared state moves to the nearest common owner, which passes values and callbacks to coordinated children.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Shared state moves to the nearest common owner, which passes values and callbacks to coordinated children.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Two temperature inputs share one converted value owned by their common calculator parent. Your goal is to repair unsynchronized sibling state and control when form state resets. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Props and composition; javascript events model; Minimal and normalized state; Lists and stable keys. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Shared state moves to the nearest common owner, which passes values and callbacks to coordinated children. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Lifting too high increases coupling and rerenders, while independent state should remain local. This draft therefore rejects: Duplicating shared state in both children is safe if callbacks try to keep it synchronized.

includes: Place shared state at the nearest common owner and use identity or keys to preserve or reset it deliberately.
Repair unsynchronized sibling state and control when form state resets.
excludes: Lifting too high increases coupling and rerenders, while independent state should remain local.

outcomes: Repair unsynchronized sibling state and control when form state resets.

examples.title: Lifting and preserving state: concrete case
examples.situation: Two temperature inputs share one converted value owned by their common calculator parent.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Two temperature inputs share one converted value owned by their common calculator parent.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use one common owner for genuinely shared state and pass controlled values rather than synchronizing duplicate copies.
examples.result: The result follows because Use one common owner for genuinely shared state and pass controlled values rather than synchronizing duplicate copies.

examples.title: Lifting and preserving state: boundary case
examples.situation: Lifting too high increases coupling and rerenders, while independent state should remain local.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Lifting too high increases coupling and rerenders, while independent state should remain local.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Duplicating shared state in both children is safe if callbacks try to keep it synchronized.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Use one common owner for genuinely shared state and pass controlled values rather than synchronizing duplicate copies.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: Duplicating shared state in both children is safe if callbacks try to keep it synchronized.
